name: Qatar
summary: Day 41. CEASEFIRE INTERCEPTED: Qatar intercepted 7 Iranian missiles and drones within hours of ceasefire taking effect on April 8 — likely pre-ceasefire launches still in flight or IRGC regional units operating independently. Ceasefire path opens potential Ras Laffan LNG/helium restart but physical restoration takes weeks. Qatar's interest in Hormuz reopening remains existential for LNG exports — Ras Laffan still offline since Feb 28. South Pars/North Dome shared-field strike precedent intact. Pakistan-mediated Qatar LNG transit deal under IRGC three-tier system held.

signals.content: QatarEnergy halted LNG production at Ras Laffan after initial attacks. European gas (TTF) surged 45%.

Iranian missiles caused extensive damage at Ras Laffan — Pearl GTL facility and LNG Trains 4 & 6 destroyed. 17% LNG capacity gone. $20B/year revenue loss. Up to 5 years to repair. QatarEnergy shut world's largest urea plant.
Qatar expelled Iranian attachés after Ras Laffan strike. Helium supply (30% of global) cut off — one of only two semiconductor-grade helium plants in world.

IRGC threatened US university campuses in Gulf on March 29, specifically naming Texas A&M University in Qatar. Deadline: Monday March 30 noon Tehran time. Demanded US condemn bombing of two Iranian universities or face retaliation against US educational institutions. Warned employees, professors, and students to stay 1km from campuses. Separately, helium supply still offline — chip fabs on 8-day countdown (early April crunch per TrendForce).

Qatar Emir attended Jeddah Trilateral Summit with MBS and Jordan's King Abdullah (March 31) — condemned 'Iranian attacks on vital and civilian facilities.' Ras Laffan repair remains 5+ years minimum (Phil Kornbluth). QatarEnergy force majeure continues. Helium: 200 specialized containers stranded near Hormuz. Even available helium physically stuck. Rerouting via Cape of Good Hope adds 10-14 days + boil-off. Qatar's Ras Laffan damage = 30-38% of global helium removed. CONFIRMED.

CF Industries -5.6% and Cheniere -3.4% on de-escalation trade — market pricing QAFCO restart and Qatar LNG resumption if war ends. But Hormuz decoupling thesis: even if war ends, Hormuz may stay under Iranian selective control → QAFCO shipments still blocked. Qatar's structural damage (Ras Laffan 17% capacity, 5-year repair) persists regardless. European TTF pulled back to €53.73 on peace signal but €72/MWh Goldman base case for Q2. EU gas storage at 30% — lowest seasonal since pre-2022. If blockade persists through summer refill season (May-Oct), industrial shutdowns in Europe. (Sources: Eurostat, Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg, Shell)

CONFIRMED: Iran struck tanker AQUA 1 chartered to QatarEnergy, 17nm north of Ras Laffan, with 3 cruise missiles — 2 intercepted by defenses, 1 hit the vessel. 21 crew safe. IRGC claimed responsibility, alleging the vessel had Israeli ties. Iran reportedly gave Qatar advance notice — suggesting calculated escalation rather than indiscriminate attack. Second vessel attack in Gulf waters in 2 days (after Al-Salmi VLCC at Dubai). QatarEnergy issued official statement confirming the attack. Attack demonstrates Iran willing to strike near Qatar's most critical energy infrastructure. (Sources: QatarEnergy, Al Jazeera, AGBI)

CONFIRMED: Al Jazeera English/Arabic editorial divergence continues — MEMRI analysis confirmed English broadcasts more balanced while Arabic output more Iran-sympathetic. Qatar hedging by audience. Despite Ras Laffan damage and AQUA 1 attack on QatarEnergy vessel, Qatar positioning as mediator-sympathizer. Jeddah Trilateral Summit (Qatar Emir, MBS, King Abdullah) produced joint condemnation of Iranian attacks on civilian infrastructure. (Sources: Al Jazeera, MEMRI)

AQUA 1 tanker (Panama-flagged, QatarEnergy-chartered) struck by Iranian cruise missile April 1 — 17nm north of Ras Laffan, all 21 crew safe. Two other missiles intercepted. IRGC alleged Israeli ties to vessel. Reports indicate Iran gave Qatar advance notice — calculated escalation. This is the first strike on a QatarEnergy-chartered vessel since Ras Laffan damage on March 18. Qatar FM condemned the attack. Qatar now hit on both facilities (Ras Laffan March 18) and shipping (AQUA 1 April 1). (Sources: QatarEnergy, Al Jazeera, AGBI)

Oman-Iran Hormuz transit talks reported (The National). Qatar LNG still offline — Ras Laffan 5-year repair timeline unchanged. OPEC+ confirmed 206K bpd increase for May but Qatar cannot deliver through Hormuz. Qatar's role as mediator complicated by being both a strike target (AQUA 1 tanker April 1, Ras Laffan March 18) and maintaining Iran relations. 45-day ceasefire framework under discussion (Axios) but Qatar not listed among mediators (Pakistan, Egypt, Turkey). Qatar's diplomatic position increasingly awkward — condemned Iran at Jeddah Trilateral but still hedging via Al Jazeera Arabic editorial line.

Israeli strike on Iran's South Pars petrochemical plant directly affects shared South Pars/North Dome gas field — Iran side struck, Qatar side unaffected but the precedent of striking shared resource infrastructure escalates the risk calculus for all Gulf producers. Separately, former FM Zarif published 'Beyond Ceasefire' roadmap in Foreign Affairs (enrichment below 3.67%, regional fuel consortium, Hormuz-for-sanctions). Gulf states publicly rejected it — UAE's Gargash said it 'ignores aggression against neighbors,' former Qatari PM warned Iran of 'loss of friends.' Iran's 10-clause ceasefire counter-proposal includes Hormuz protocol. (Sources: Euronews, i24News, Al Jazeera)

SOUTH PARS / QATAR SHARED FIELD RISK — UNDERREPORTED [CONFIRMED + ANALYTICAL]. The South Pars gas field that Israel struck on April 6 is shared with Qatar (where the Qatari side is called the North Field/North Dome). Qatar produces ~77 million tons of LNG annually from this field — making it the world's #1 LNG exporter alongside the US. The geological structure is shared. While Israel struck the Iranian petrochemical plant, NOT the gas reservoir, the precedent is alarming for Qatar: (1) reservoir-shared infrastructure under fire; (2) Qatar's $200B+ LNG expansion (currently underway) is geographically tied to this field; (3) any escalation that damages the gas reservoir itself would be a geological catastrophe shared by both nations; (4) Qatar mediated some of the early ceasefire talks; Qatar has the most to lose from infrastructure escalation in this specific basin. Western media is focused on Iran's loss; the Qatar exposure angle has been buried. Structural risk to global LNG supply that markets are NOT pricing. Qatar's silence on the strike on the geological structure it depends on is conspicuous. Two Qatar LNG tankers were quietly allowed through Hormuz under a Pakistan-brokered secret deal — confirming Qatar's acute sensitivity to maintaining LNG flows.

EVENING UPDATE: SABIC Jubail fire after Iranian missile debris fell near facilities — while geographically Saudi, the precedent of collateral energy infrastructure damage extends risk to all Gulf producers. Ras Laffan repair timeline still 5 years. No helium supply update. OPEC+ 206K bpd for May conditional on Hormuz — Qatar cannot deliver. Iran's 10-point counter-proposal includes Hormuz protocol potentially benefiting Qatar transit. (Sources: Arab News, Bloomberg)

CEASEFIRE OPENS PATH FOR QATAR LNG/HELIUM RESTART [REPORTED]. The 14-day ceasefire framework — if it holds — opens a path for restart of Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG and helium production that has been disrupted since the IRGC strikes. South Korea sources 64.7% of helium from Qatar. KOSPI/Samsung/SK Hynix surged on the ceasefire largely because the helium-cliff scenario (Day 1 of TrendForce April 7-10 production-cut window) is fading. However, physical restart of damaged Ras Laffan facilities takes weeks at minimum. Qatar's silence on the ceasefire is conspicuous — Qatar has been absorbing the worst LNG/helium disruption of any non-belligerent. The Pakistan-mediated secret deal that allowed Qatar LNG tankers to transit Hormuz under the IRGC three-tier system likely played a role in the broader Pakistan-as-mediator arc.

Qatar intercepted 7 Iranian missiles and drones within hours of ceasefire taking effect on April 8. Assessment: likely pre-ceasefire launches still in flight or IRGC regional units operating independently rather than deliberate post-ceasefire targeting of Qatar. Qatar's interest in Hormuz reopening remains existential for LNG exports — Ras Laffan still offline since Feb 28. (Source: CNBC, Al Jazeera)

edges.context: Qatar intercepted 7 Iranian missiles/drones during ceasefire — likely pre-ceasefire launches in flight or IRGC units operating independently. Ras Laffan still offline. Hormuz reopening existential for Qatar LNG exports.
Ceasefire path opens potential Ras Laffan restart but physical restoration takes weeks. Pakistan-Qatar secret transit deal under IRGC three-tier system held throughout the war.

Ras Laffan damage = 30-38% of global helium removed. 200 specialized containers stranded near Hormuz. AQUA 1 attack near Ras Laffan reinforces that even ceasefire may not restore shipments if Iran continues Gulf-wide strikes. Fabs at 50% allocations.

QatarEnergy shut world's largest urea plant. CF Industries -5.6% on de-escalation trade pricing QAFCO restart. But Hormuz decoupling means even ceasefire may not restore shipments. Urea prices surged 40% ($500→$700/mt).

trigger_points.condition: Ras Laffan repair timeline extends beyond 5 years
trigger_points.mechanism: Would make 17% LNG capacity loss semi-permanent. Europe would need to find alternative supply. Helium shortage becomes structural. Fertilizer crisis deepens.
